rationale_for_baseline_tier: Mild functional dyspepsia of 4 weeks, acutely a little worse for a day, with NO alarm features, non-cardiac food-related pain and (on the sealed clinician record) normal observations and a soft non-peritonitic abdomen. This is a benign, telehealth/GP-manageable presentation: an empirical proton pump inhibitor trial, dietary modification and a routine GP review within 1-2 weeks for H. pylori testing. T1 (routine GP appointment within 1-2 weeks) is the correct tier. The source note's urgent after-hours in-person escalation over-triages the true acuity; there is no unexcludable serious mimic requiring urgent in-person management once the history has screened cardiac, biliary and bleeding causes as negative.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, or passing black tarry stools (GI bleeding)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — go to an emergency department; call 000 if bleeding heavily, faint or lightheaded
escalation_edges.rationale: Overt upper-GI bleeding indicates a complicated ulcer or malignancy and needs emergency assessment and endoscopy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you vomit blood or something that looks like coffee grounds, or your stools turn black and tarry, go to an emergency department straight away — call 000 if you also feel faint or the bleeding is heavy.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe, rigid or board-like pain across the whole abdomen
escalation_edges.rationale: A sudden rigid abdomen suggests a perforated viscus or acute surgical abdomen — a time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sudden, severe pain that makes your whole tummy rigid and board-like, call 000 immediately — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The pain changes to crushing chest pain spreading to the arm, neck or jaw, with sweating or breathlessness
escalation_edges.rationale: A change to an exertional/radiating pain with autonomic features would raise acute coronary syndrome, which is a 000 emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain changes and becomes a crushing chest pain that spreads to your arm, neck or jaw, or comes with sweating or shortness of breath, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New difficulty swallowing, food sticking, persistent vomiting, or unintentional weight loss
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: See a GP promptly (within days) for urgent assessment and endoscopy referral
escalation_edges.rationale: New dysphagia or weight loss are alarm features requiring prompt (same-week) GP assessment and endoscopy referral.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start having trouble swallowing, food gets stuck, you keep vomiting, or you lose weight without trying, see your GP promptly to arrange a camera test of the stomach.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Symptoms not improving after the proton pump inhibitor trial, or recurring after it stops
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Routine GP review to organise H. pylori testing and consider endoscopy
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent or relapsing dyspepsia warrants H. pylori test-and-treat and consideration of endoscopy, but on a routine (non-urgent) footing.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the burning and fullness don't settle after the 4-week medicine course, or come straight back, book a routine GP appointment to test for the H. pylori stomach bug and decide whether a camera test is needed.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is anxious because the discomfort is high up near the chest
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, written safety-netting the patient can refer to alone, and explicitly reassure that the history has screened out cardiac and surgical causes — while naming the exact warning signs that should prompt calling 000 or going to an emergency department. Living alone lowers the threshold to advise calling 000 (rather than self-transport) if a red-flag symptom appears.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your symptoms fit indigestion that can be managed at home and with your regular GP — this does not need an emergency or urgent in-person visit. Start the acid-lowering medicine (pantoprazole) 30 minutes before breakfast, eat five small meals a day instead of three big ones, avoid anti-inflammatory painkillers like ibuprofen, and cut down on smoking and caffeine. Most people improve within a few days. Book a routine GP appointment in the next 1-2 weeks to review how you are going and arrange a test for the H. pylori stomach bug.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP appointment (telehealth or in person) within 1-2 weeks; a pharmacist can advise on antacids in the meantime.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and you only have the usual mild discomfort, an antacid and smaller meals are reasonable until your GP is open — but use the emergency warning signs below at any time.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Uninvestigated dyspepsia, no alarm features, non-cardiac food-related epigastric burning with early satiation and bloating. Empirical PPI trial started; please review response, arrange H. pylori breath/stool testing (test-and-treat), and refer for gastroscopy if symptoms persist beyond the trial or any alarm feature appears.
